sentences.text: Phyllis Cooper Young.
Maiden-- my maiden name first and last?
Or just--?
Polish, or--?
Polish.
Fela, F-E-L-A, Rynska, R-Y-N-S-K-A.
Right.
No.
I was born August 12, 1923.
In a city in Poland named Sosnowiec.
S-O-S-N-O-W-I-E-C.
Yeah.
Katowice.
My father's name was Kalman.
And my mother's name was Frymeta.
I had an older sister.
Manya.
Well, if I-- being the age of I don't know, 16, 17-- I maybe began to understand, you know, a little bit more about the life.
I think that in maybe only in Sosnowiec, but I think that throughout Poland, the Jewish people-- I don't think there was a middle class.
You were either very comfortable or poor.
Businesses, merchants, those were the people who-- who had the money.
My father had several.
I can't remember them all.
But one I remember, he used to make the tops to the shoes, not the whole shoe.
And I don't know what happened to that.
I-- I guess it didn't work out or whatever the case was.
He went into some real estate.
Now, when I say real estate, it's not like-- like real estate now.
Somebody had a piece of land, small little piece of land, and he would, you know, negotiate with someone who wants to buy it, and that's how he would make a living.
I went to public school, which was-- I don't remember-- six or seven grades.
It was a school for Jewish girls.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the education-- I must say, surprisingly, for a country like Poland-- it was pretty, pretty good.
We had Jewish teachers.
And I don't know from being here, you know, but I felt like-- for instance, we were getting some beginner's chemistry, let's say starting at fifth grade.
They would take us to a lab.
It wasn't a very, you know, up to date-- whatever they had in those days.
And we were actually would experiment.
No, you say it good.
You say it OK.
I guess so.
I really guess so, because that's where I went to.
I know we went-- I think the boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the boys went to yeshivas, you know.
My father was orthodox.
But in those days, you really wouldn't-- he was clean shaven, and that was like--
Right, right.
That's how you called it.
Very religious.
I mean, all the holidays.
Shabbos, my father wouldn't write.
He wouldn't handle money, went to shul.
And although my sister and I were beginning to sort of like drift away a little bit from it.
See, we lived like, in the suburb, like outside the city.
And we would want to go get together with friends who lived in the city.
We had trolley cars.
Maybe I shouldn't even say it, but sometimes, she and I, we cheated, because it was a big walk.
If Father had found out, he would have killed us.
Oh yeah.
Oh yeah.
She would have been upset too, but I think he was more or less the--
Cheder. .
When I was small child, I went to cheder.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
I guess in the steeple, when they were-- where they went to daven.
You know.
No.
Because, you know, the men and the women sat separately.
The only time we would go in, I think, Yom Kippur.
Yeah, there was synagogues.
But where I lived in the outside of the city, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were these-- [? mostly these ?] steeples.
OK.
I wanted to go to what we called gymnasium-- high school.
But you had to pay.
And my father couldn't afford it.
My sister on the other hand, she was brilliant.
She won a scholarship.
And she went to high school, gymnasium, or whatever you want to call it.
I couldn't make it.
I was good, and I don't mean to say it, that I want to-- I was a good student, but not good enough to get a scholarship like she got.
And so I took some courses, correspondence courses, for secretarial work.
But I never got a chance to even use it, because I was 16 years old when the war broke out.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Gordoni.
Gordoni was a little bit, I think, to the leftist, if I remember correctly.
Zionist, but a little bit to the leftist.
I just saw it mentioned in the Jewish journal.
There were a lot of them.
There was a Betar.
That was HaShomer HaTzair, HaTzair, a lot of them.
And this is actually our social life was at these--
--At these organizations, the boys and the girls.
My father, from what I remember, not myself-- I was-- I was too young to even, you know, have such thoughts.
But I remember that my father was planning on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emigrating to France.
But he got sick.
He needed surgery or some-- and the money went.
Then, a few years, a couple of years before the war, I think, broke out, he was talking about Israel.
Palestine then, of course, and they closed the quota.
Yeah.
Yeah, he would've gone.
You know, there was so much anti-Semitism in Poland, that one couldn't even start begin.
But I'll tell you one incident.
And that was during already, that was the night, the winter of 1940.
I was then 17 years old.
And they were predicting a snowstorm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to bed that night, and I said a prayer.
I said, dear God, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] let me wake up in the morning.
Let there be no snow and no Nazis.
He granted me one wish, no snow.
Now, you'd like to know, why snow?
What's so bad about snow?
Because people, kids that I used to go to school with-- I mean, I didn't go to school with, but we grew up together, next door, played, you know--
Kids don't know prejudice.
Kids are taught by their parents, or racism, what they call racism today.
They take a rock, and wrap the snow around it and throw it at you.
Yeah, Gentile
Yeah, yeah, Polish.
Yeah.
That's the pre-war years I'm talking-- well, this was an incident.
There was always anti-Semitism in Poland, always.
Yeah, yeah we played together.
You know, kids get into fights.
But it was always zyd, zyd, zyd, they called us.
This is the Jew for-- and other words that I wouldn't even know how to translate into English.
Well, not only did we hear, but we had some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] proof, so to speak.
Now, you can ask me how.
You see, my city that I came from, we were very close to the German border.
I think we must have been about 15 miles from Auschwitz, which was Oswiecim in Polish.
Auschwitz--
--was Oswiecim in Polish.
So that's the region that I came from.
So when Hitler came into power-- I can't go back that far-- but I'm talking about mid '30s, already closer, maybe '37, '38.
And he was getting rid of some Jews, sent them into Poland.
Well, they would tells us that what's going in Germany, how they hate the Jews, and how they take away their homes, their businesses, how they literally kill them, whatever they do.
And I don't know.
I don't know whether we really-- well, we did believe it.
Because why would people--?
The German Jew-- Jews as a rule, to my knowledge, they were pretty successful.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why would they just all of a sudden pick up and leave homeland, a land that they were born, their kids were-- How did the Jews in your town react to these stories?
People didn't believe it.
That was the whole thing.
They settled, like I say, in our town, the Katowice.
See, that was basically-- this-- this part of Poland, I think during World War I or before, it was part of Germany.
And they still, some of the state, not the Jews, but I mean, the non-Jews, they spoke German.
You know, so it was easier for them to live there, because they like walked from one, you know, into something that are was easier for them to settle in.
I'm sure there were, but the to be honest with you, I was too young to pay attention to this stuff.
I can't remember.
I was at home.
And I happened to be up six o'clock in the morning.
Was a beautiful, glorious day.
The sun was shining.
And I heard the plane overhead, looked up, and Hitler attacked Poland.
I saw the plane.
I didn't see any bombing or anything like this.
I heard the-- like shots, like maybe they were-- the Polish army-- that was another joke.
They were, I guess, maybe trying to shoot it down, or whatever.
But I ran in back into the house.
What happened next?
Next, we were listening to the radios.
And we were told that Hitler attacked Poland.
For the next couple of days, of course, we were very upset.
Well, actually, we knew it was coming.
Because that August, the whole month of August, that was the whole topic, you know, that whatever was happening, Hitler-- this one trying to make a deal with him.
This one and that one.
We knew that it's going to happen.
Oh, yeah.
Oh sure.
The Jewish men were drafted to the Polish, absolutely.
They were in the Polish army.
No, because we were two girls.
I don't remember any of my uncles.
I think they were too old.
But the next couple of days, it was like, just sitting there listening to the radio and how they were attacking, and how they were almost in Warsaw, and you know, stuff like that.
People started running, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] packing up their belongings, and going-- they figured that going deeper into Poland-- to Poland.
Because we were so close to the--
And I understand.

How true it is, I don't know, but I understand that when the German army started marching into Poland, especially through the region where I lived, the sympathizers-- and Polish army was retreating-- the sympathizers were throwing down hot oil on them, on the Polish army.
The Volk-- that's right, the Volksdeutsche.
That's right.
Well, I was just getting to it, because see, people started running.
Everybody, whatever they could-- the wagon or something, a push cart, or whatever, they loaded up things.
And they ran to these villages and stuff.
My mother, I think, said to my father-- because I told you he came from a village that was a little further from where we lived, deeper into Poland.
My father said, I'm not going anywhere.
If I'm going to die, I want to die in my own house.
That was Friday morning that they invaded.
Saturday, Sunday night, because we were already hearing on the radio that they're nearing.
They were not far from us at all.
We spent the whole night in an attic, the whole family.
And we heard them.
We didn't see them.
But we heard them, because of the tanks, the trucks, and the shootings.
They just-- the Nazis--
My family and some other neighbors, because we lived in an apartment building.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And shooting-- we couldn't see anything, but we heard it.
It seems we found out that they just-- coming into the city, they just shot whoever they saw in the streets.
They didn't ask questions.
They were shooting.
Well, we eventually had to come down from the attic.
We couldn't stay there.
And then, they just went on a rampage.
They lined up the Jews.
And they went one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, 10, boom.
Just like this.
Shot.
They shot them.
That's also a very good question.
The Polish people, some of them helped them out.
OK?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Listen, for them to-- in those days, to pull a man's pants down, and they knew right then and there that they're dealing with Jews.
It's a very common thing.
But I can't-- I mean, I don't remember exactly everything that happened after that.
And they set up a government provisional, whatever.
Well, curfew.
First of all we have had a curfew.
The time?
No, I can't remember.
And started with the arm bands.
We had white with the blue star of David.
It's funny, because in some places they wore the yellow star.
I don't know how they determined this.
If I knew that, I would-- and then they formed the Jewish committee.
You know, they did forms.
Yeah.
Yeah, the judenrate.
And, you know, life went on.
No.
Well, the Nazis gave them orders, what to do, what they wanted done.
And they had to follow.
I wasn't there long enough to answer that question.
I heard a lot about this.
I have read a lot about it.
I have a book here. is the name of the book.
And there's also a lot about-- some of it about my city, and the judenrate, but all this happened after I was taken away.
Not so much.
Things were off, there's no question about it.
Food became very scarce.
And if you knew somebody, like we knew somebody that had a bakery, so we knew we would have the bread, and stuff.
It was rough.
There is no question about it.
Oh yeah.
That's something that always exists, always existed.
Not at this time, not by me.
Nothing.
You see now, why I feel guilty, so guilty, because I avoided all this.
We were able to get around in the street, as long as I followed the curfew, and wore my arm band.
And if you came across a guy that wanted to rape you or beat you up, you had to take it and go on.
I mean, but it was like, it was-- I can't describe it.
You were just going on.
It wasn't-- you were just going on.
Well, my sister had gotten married early.
And she lived like in the city.
So I would go to her, you know.
We were able to walk, because it wasn't that far.
I would go to her, visit with her.
Friends of mine, you know, we try to stay together and--
Not at this point.
You have to understand that I was taken away so early, that I really don't have the answers to all these questions, because all this came later on.
I mean, it's like, how long can we take this?
I've hadn't-- when they took me away, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was no ghetto.
I mean, we all lived in our own apartments.
As a matter of fact, they were looking for people to work, because, you see, this is my-- how I figured this-- Hitler attacked Russia in 1941, June '41.
And Hitler needed man power, because he drafted every able-bodied man.
He drafted.
My father was too old.
Too old.
Well, the same thing in the steeple, and hoping that they don't fall in on you, you know?
Like everything else was done, you--
It wasn't permitted.
It was forbidden, but they-- and if you couldn't do it, he did it, he prayed in the house.
He'd [INAUDIBLE] in the house.
They'd be shot.
No.
I myself did not witness any shootings.
Not then.
Not then.
Sometimes.
They had like a raid, you know.
They would ride down the street, and pick up a boys, girls.
But what they did with them, I don't know.
Some did, some didn't.
I was taken November 3, 1941.
OK.
I was asleep, seven o'clock in the morning.
I heard knocking on the door.
Open up the door, there were two SS men.
They said-- they asked me my name.
Yes.
Get dressed and come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the next door neighbor, friend of mine, they knocked on her door too.
It seems to me that at that time from what I heard, that see, like I said, they were employing some people.
They needed man power.
And who better than the Jew?
All right, Phyllis--
So I wasn't working.
Had I been working, maybe I would have remained longer.
